title: Polenta Morbida
subtitle: Soft, creamy polenta with Parmesan and butter — Northern Italy's most comforting side
description: Polenta morbida is the Italian equivalent of creamed mashed potato: ground maize cooked slowly in salted water until it swells and thickens, then finished with generous butter and Parmesan. It is the foundation under braised meats, stews, mushrooms, and slow-cooked sauces across Northern Italy. Good polenta requires time and stirring — the instant variety is a different product. This recipe uses medium-ground polenta and takes forty minutes of occasional attention.
sourceNotes: Polenta has been the staple grain of Northern Italy since maize arrived from the Americas in the sixteenth century. The morbida (soft) preparation is standard in Lombardy, Veneto, and Friuli. Pellegrino Artusi's La Scienza in Cucina (1891) contains detailed polenta instructions consistent with modern technique.

recipeTools.notes: A heavy-based pan prevents scorching during the long, slow cook. A copper or cast-iron pot is traditional but any thick-bottomed pan works.
Long-handled — the polenta bubbles and spits as it thickens.
For the initial whisking as the polenta first goes in, to prevent lumps.

glossaryTerms.definition: Ground dried maize (corn), cooked with water into a thick porridge. Medium or coarse ground gives better texture than fine-ground. 'Instant' polenta is pre-cooked and produces a different, pastier result.

ingredients.notes: Not instant — look for 'bramata' or medium-ground on the packet
Or a mix of water and whole milk for extra richness

steps: Bring the water to the boil in a large heavy-based pan. Add the salt.
Once boiling, reduce the heat to medium-low. Pour the polenta into the water in a steady, thin stream, whisking constantly as you pour to prevent lumps.
Switch to a wooden spoon. The polenta will begin to thicken almost immediately. Stir continuously for the first 5 minutes.

Reduce the heat to the lowest possible setting. Cook for a further 35–40 minutes, stirring every 4–5 minutes, scraping the bottom and sides of the pan to prevent sticking. The polenta is done when it pulls away cleanly from the sides and a spoon dragged through it leaves a clear trail that holds for a few seconds.
Remove from the heat. Add the cold butter cubes and the Parmesan and beat vigorously until both are fully incorporated and the polenta is glossy and smooth. Taste and adjust seasoning.
Serve immediately — polenta stiffens as it cools. If needed, thin with a splash of hot water and stir to restore the consistency.

content.text: takes time. The forty-minute cook is not optional — it is what transforms raw, starchy grain into something smooth and deeply savoury. Polenta is forgiving of inattention (stir every few minutes rather than constantly after the first five), but it rewards the time investment.

Bring {{water}} of water to the boil in a large, heavy-based pan. Add 1.5 tsp of salt. Reduce the heat to medium-low. Pour {{polenta-medium-ground}} of polenta into the water in a slow, steady stream, whisking constantly with your other hand. Switch to a wooden spoon once all the polenta is in. Stir continuously for the first 5 minutes as it thickens.

Reduce to the lowest heat. Cook for 35–40 minutes, stirring every 4–5 minutes and scraping the bottom of the pan each time to prevent any scorching. The polenta is ready when it pulls away cleanly from the sides and a trail left by the spoon holds its shape for a few seconds.

Remove from the heat. Add {{butter-unsalted}} of cold butter in cubes and {{parmesan}} of grated Parmesan. Beat vigorously until both are fully incorporated and the polenta is glossy. Taste and adjust seasoning. Serve immediately — polenta stiffens quickly as it cools. A splash of hot water and a vigorous stir restores it if needed.

Polenta morbida is most commonly served as the base for something braised or saucy: sautéed mushrooms, a slow-cooked ragu, braised sausages, or chicken alla cacciatora. Spread it in a wide, shallow bowl and ladle the topping over. It is also excellent cold — left to set in a tin, sliced, and pan-fried in butter until golden on both sides.
